text: The robotic right hemicolectomy is performed using the Da Vinci SI system with standard port placement. The transverse colon, transposed between the liver and diaphragm, is visualized and reduced back into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon begins dissection at the ileocolic pedicle using monopolar cautery to open tissue planes and identify the correct dissection plane beneath the ileocolic vessel. Hemlock clips are applied to ligate and divide the ileocolic vessels, with the endo-wrist instruments facilitating precise dissection around the vessels to prevent bleeding. The ileocolic artery and vein are isolated and divided separately using robotic hemlock clips.

The surgeon uses robotic arms to dissect under the right mesocolon, separating the durinum from the mesocolon. The robotic system provides counter-traction by pulling down Girota's fascia, facilitating access to the lesser sac. The entrance into the lesser sac is clearly visible, aided by three-dimensional vision and endo-wrist instruments.
The surgeon uses an energy sealing device to divide the omentum and mesocolon, with the endo-wrist vessel sealer facilitating a quick dissection without visible blood loss.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the hepatic flexure of the colon by detaching the hepatocolic ligaments. The lateral attachments of the right colon are completely released. Tension is applied to the small bowel, which is divided using the vessel sealing device along the line of transection. A window is created next to the transverse colon at the proposed site of division, and the mesocolon is divided in a straight line up to that level.

The surgeon assesses the perfusion of the colon and small bowel using fluorescence with indocyanine green (ICG), observing real-time perfusion. A robotic endo-wrist stapler is then used to divide the transverse colon and small bowel at the chosen site of division, ensuring secure transection.

The surgeon performs an isoparastaltic side-to-side anastomosis by approximating the small bowel to the transverse colon. A stage suture is placed on the small bowel, followed by enterotomy and colotomy using monopolar scissors. The stapler is inserted, and traction on the stage suture facilitates feeding the colon and small bowel into the stapler, creating a secure anastomosis. The enterotomy is closed with a running suture in two layers using robotic instruments, ensuring precise suture placement and minimal tissue trauma.
The surgeon performs a second layer of suturing to bury the initial suture layer, creating a watertight anastomosis during the intracorporeal anastomosis procedure.

Following the completion of the anastomosis, a bolus of indocyanine green (ICG) is administered to reassess perfusion, showing excellent blood flow in both the small bowel and colon. The surgical specimen is retrieved through a four-centimeter suprapubic incision.
